systems.name: Movable Shape Voicing System
systems.summary: The book's central architecture is the movable chord shape: every non-open voicing is a transposable template that can be slid up or down the neck to any root, with a two-fret shift equaling a whole-step transposition. This principle multiplies the dictionary's utility across all twelve keys from a single learned fingering. The system operates over a taxonomy of voicing categories (drop 2, drop 3, shell, extended, altered, inversion, open, quartal), each governed by rules about how to select, move, and modify shapes in a comping context.

members.name: Drop 2 Chord (Root Position)
members.summary: Four-note voicing built by dropping the second-highest note of a closed-position chord down an octave; introduced as a foundational jazz guitar voicing.

members.name: Drop 3 Chord (Root Position)
members.summary: Four-note voicing built by dropping the third-highest note of a closed-position chord down an octave; paired with drop 2 as a foundational shape.

members.name: Shell Chord (Guide Tone Chord)
members.summary: Three-note voicing containing root, 3rd, and 7th (or 6th); captures essential harmonic identity with minimal note count on lower string sets.

members.name: Extended Chord
members.summary: Chord incorporating intervals beyond the root, 3rd, 5th, and 7th (e.g., 9, 11, 13), requiring deliberate omission choices on guitar.

members.name: Major 6 Chord
members.summary: Extended chord where the 6 replaces the 7; does not contain the 7th.

members.name: Major 13 Chord
members.summary: Extended chord containing both the 7 and the 6 (=13), distinguished from major 6 by the presence of the 7th.

members.name: Altered Dominant 9#11 (Lydian Dominant)
members.summary: Dominant chord with #11 extension derived from the Lydian dominant scale; characteristically functions as bVII7 resolving to Imaj7.

members.name: Altered Dominant 7b5 (Altered Scale)
members.summary: Dominant chord with b5 (and b9 or #9) derived from the altered scale; characteristically functions as V7alt resolving to Im7 or Imaj7.

members.name: Root-Position Inversion
members.summary: Chord voicing with the root in the bass voice.

members.name: First Inversion (3rd in Bass)
members.summary: Chord voicing with the 3rd in the bass voice.

members.name: Second Inversion (5th in Bass)
members.summary: Chord voicing with the 5th in the bass voice.

members.name: Third Inversion (7th in Bass)
members.summary: Chord voicing with the 7th in the bass voice.

members.name: Open Chord
members.summary: Voicing that incorporates open strings; limited to guitar-friendly key centers but delivers unique resonant sonority.

members.name: Quartal Chord (4th Chord)
members.summary: Chord built by stacking 4th intervals above the lowest note rather than thirds; sounds open and modern, applicable over any jazz standard.

members.name: Quartal Chord — String Set 2-3-4-5
members.summary: Quartal voicings derived from the C Dorian scale positioned on string set 2-3-4-5.

members.name: Quartal Chord — String Set 1-2-3-4
members.summary: Quartal voicings derived from the C Dorian scale transposed to string set 1-2-3-4.

traversal_rules.name: Movable Shape Transposition Rule
traversal_rules.summary: Any non-open chord shape can be transposed to a new root by sliding it up or down the neck; a two-fret shift equals a whole-step transposition.

references.quote_excerpt: By moving the chord shapes up or down the guitar neck, you get other chords of the same chord type
move the chord shape of a Cmaj7 voicing 2 frets higher and it becomes a Dmaj7 voicing

traversal_rules.name: Inversion Comping Substitution Rule
traversal_rules.summary: Any inversion of a chord symbol (root, 3rd, 5th, or 7th in bass) may be freely substituted for that symbol in comping.

references.quote_excerpt: Each of these chord inversions can be applied to that chord in your comping

traversal_rules.name: Ear-Based Inversion Selection
traversal_rules.summary: Among available inversions of a chord symbol, ear-based judgment determines which voicing fits the musical context, as each inversion produces a different timbral flavor.

references.quote_excerpt: each inversion will have a slightly different sound and some voicings will work better than others
putting the root or the 3rd in the bass causes the chord to have a slightly different flavor

traversal_rules.name: Quartal String Set Position Transfer
traversal_rules.summary: Quartal voicings built on string set 2-3-4-5 can be transferred to string set 1-2-3-4 to access the same harmonic material in a higher register.

references.quote_excerpt: Quartal Chords Based on the C Dorian Scale (String Set 2-3-4-5)
Quartal Chords Based on the C Dorian Scale (String Set 1-2-3-4)

modification_rules.name: Shell Chord Minimum Density (Root + 3rd + 7th)
modification_rules.summary: A shell chord must contain at minimum the root, 3rd, and 7th (or 6th) to preserve harmonic identity; this three-note floor is sufficient without the 5th.

references.quote_excerpt: These three-note chords contain the root, 3rd, 7th (or the 6th) of each chord

modification_rules.name: Major 6 Chord: 6 Replaces 7 (Omit 7th)
modification_rules.summary: In a major 6 chord, the 6th replaces the 7th; the 7th must be omitted. This distinguishes the major 6 from all other extended chords, which retain the 7th.

references.quote_excerpt: In a major 6 chord, the 6 replaces the 7
they don't contain a 7. All the other extended chords (such as maj9), contain the 7th

modification_rules.name: Major 13 Chord: Retain Both 7th and 6th
modification_rules.summary: A major 13 chord must contain both the 7th and the 6 (=13); if the 7th is absent, the chord is a major 6, not a major 13.

references.quote_excerpt: When a 6 and a 7 are both present in a major chord, it becomes a major 13 chord

modification_rules.name: Guitar Voicing Omission Policy
modification_rules.summary: Because guitar has fewer available simultaneous notes than piano, extended chords require deliberate note omissions; the player must observe which intervals are replaced in each voicing.

references.quote_excerpt: guitarists have to leave some notes out when adding extended notes to their chord shapes

modification_rules.name: Altered Dominant Extension: Scale-Origin Assignment
modification_rules.summary: The #11 extension is assigned to the Lydian dominant scale (bVII7 function) while the b5 extension is assigned to the altered scale (V7alt function); enharmonically identical pitches receive different names and harmonic roles based on parent scale.

references.quote_excerpt: Both are the same note, an f# (=gb) in C7
this chord is derived from the Lydian dominant scale (4th mode of the melodic minor scale)
this chord is derived from the altered scale (7th mode of the melodic minor scale)

modification_rules.name: Shell Chord Ensemble Tone Constraint (Soft Tone)
modification_rules.summary: Shell chords placed on lower string sets must be played with soft tone to avoid frequency clashing with the bass player in ensemble contexts.

references.quote_excerpt: you need to keep your tone in mind so you don't clash with the bass player
With a soft tone, these chords are highly effective when comping in a duo, trio, or larger ensembles

modification_rules.name: Open Chord Key-Center Restriction
modification_rules.summary: Open chord voicings are restricted to guitar-friendly key centers due to their reliance on open strings; they are not transposable like movable shapes.

references.topic: Open chords — key limitations and comping use
references.quote_excerpt: Though they are limited to a few keys, these shapes add new flavor to your comping in particular key centers

modification_rules.name: Quartal Chord Universal Applicability
modification_rules.summary: Quartal chords are not restricted to specific harmonic contexts; they can be applied over any jazz standard in both comping and soloing.

references.quote_excerpt: can be used over any jazz standard in your comping and solos

preferences.name: Prefer Four-Note Voicings for Ear Training
preferences.summary: Four-note drop 2 and drop 3 shapes should be learned before smaller shapes because they train the jazz sound in the ear more effectively despite being technically harder.

references.quote_excerpt: these four-note shapes drill the sound of jazz into your ears better than the smaller shapes

preferences.name: Learn One Shape Then Apply to a Tune
preferences.summary: After learning one shape per chord type in isolation, immediately apply it to a real jazz tune to integrate systematic vocabulary with musical ear training.

references.quote_excerpt: Start by learning one shape for each chord type then take those changes to a jazz tune you know

preferences.name: Introduce Altered Extensions in Minor Keys First
preferences.summary: Players new to altered dominant extensions should begin using them in minor key progressions (minor ii-V-I) before applying them broadly over any dominant 7th chord.

references.quote_excerpt: if you're new to these sounds start with using them in minor keys
if you have a minor ii V | chord progression (DM7b5-G7-Cm7), you can use altered extensions over the G7 chord
